title: Southern cornbread
subtitle: Buttermilk polenta quick bread baked in a square tin
excerpt: A dense, slightly crumbly American cornbread made with polenta and plain flour, leavened with baking powder and the acid in buttermilk. Mixed in one bowl and baked in 22 minutes. Good warm from the oven alongside chilli, pulled pork, or eaten plain with butter.
sourceNotes: Cornbread is a documented American quick bread with roots in Native American cooking traditions. Nineteenth-century American domestic cookbooks, many available through the Internet Archive and Project Gutenberg, document a cornmeal-and-buttermilk batter leavened with bicarbonate of soda or baking powder, baked in a greased pan or skillet. The specific combination of polenta (coarse cornmeal), buttermilk, and a small amount of wheat flour for structure is recorded in American household cooking manuals of the mid-nineteenth century. The buttermilk variant produces a more tender crumb than a water-based cornbread.

content.text: Southern cornbread is a quick bread: no yeast, no proving, no waiting. The batter goes together in one bowl and into a hot tin. The key is that the tin is already hot when the batter hits it, which crisps the base immediately. Without this the base stays soft and the cornbread loses its texture contrast between the crisp exterior and the tender crumb.

items.name: Unsalted butter
items.prepNote: melted, plus extra for the tin

content.text: Preheat the oven to 200°C fan. Butter the square tin generously and place it in the oven for 5 minutes to heat through.

In a large bowl, combine the {{polenta}}, {{plain-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{caster-sugar}}. In a separate bowl or jug, whisk the {{eggs}}, {{buttermilk}}, and melted {{unsalted-butter}} together. Pour the wet ingredients into the dry and stir until just combined. The batter will be thick and slightly lumpy. This is the
at work: the buttermilk and baking powder have already begun reacting, so move quickly.

Pour the batter into the hot tin. It will sizzle. Bake for 20 to 22 minutes until the top is firm and the edges have pulled away from the tin slightly. A skewer inserted into the centre should come out clean. The base should sound hollow when you tap the tin. Cool in the tin for 10 minutes before cutting into squares. Serve warm.
